articles.label: Investigation
articles.headline: Escambia County's $2.1M no-bid contract with commissioner's former employer draws scrutiny
articles.dek: Public records show the waste management deal bypassed competitive bidding. The commissioner says he recused himself. Meeting minutes tell a different story.
brief: Escambia County awarded a $2.1 million waste management contract to Gulf South Environmental Services without competitive bidding in January 2026.
Commissioner Dale Hendricks worked as a regional manager for Gulf South from 2018 to 2022, according to his financial disclosure forms.
Hendricks claims he recused himself from the vote, but official meeting minutes show he was present and voting when the contract was approved 4-1.
articles.body:
The contract approval took eleven minutes. No public comment. No discussion of alternatives. On January 9, 2026, the Escambia County Board of Commissioners voted to award Gulf South Environmental Services a $2.1 million contract for hazardous waste disposal at county facilities—without soliciting competing bids.

Four months later, public records obtained by The Flightline reveal a detail absent from the meeting's brief discussion: Commissioner Dale Hendricks, who voted yes, spent four years as a regional manager at Gulf South before his 2022 election to the board.

Hendricks, reached by phone last week, said he had "fully recused" himself from the matter.

"I stepped out of that vote," Hendricks said. "I made my conflicts clear to the board and to the county attorney. This is a non-story."

The official minutes from the January 9 meeting say otherwise. They record Hendricks as present for the vote and list the final tally as 4-1, with Commissioner Rosa Martinez casting the lone dissenting vote. The minutes make no mention of a recusal or abstention.

The contract

Gulf South Environmental Services, headquartered in Mobile, Alabama, provides hazardous waste collection, transport, and disposal for industrial and government clients across the Gulf Coast. The company has held smaller contracts with Escambia County in the past, most recently a $340,000 agreement in 2021 for emergency spill response.

The 2026 contract is different in scale and scope. It covers hazardous material handling at the county's public works facilities, the main jail complex, and three fire stations. The three-year agreement includes an automatic renewal clause that could extend the deal to 2032 without further board action.

Florida law allows local governments to bypass competitive bidding under specific circumstances—emergencies, sole-source situations, or contracts below certain thresholds. The county cited none of these exemptions in the January meeting. Staff presented the item under the consent agenda, a portion of meetings typically reserved for routine, non-controversial matters.

County Administrator Theresa Blackmon, in a written statement to The Flightline, said the contract "followed all applicable procurement procedures."

"Gulf South has a proven track record with Escambia County and offered competitive pricing consistent with industry standards," Blackmon wrote. She did not respond to follow-up questions about why the contract was not put out to bid.

The commissioner's history

Hendricks' connection to Gulf South is not hidden. His 2022 financial disclosure form, filed with the Escambia County Supervisor of Elections, lists his employment as "Regional Manager, Gulf South Environmental Services" from May 2018 to November 2022. He resigned from the company three weeks after winning his commission seat.

The same disclosure form lists no ongoing financial interest in the company—no stock, no consulting fees, no retirement benefits tied to company performance.

Florida's ethics laws require elected officials to abstain from votes where they have a conflict of interest, defined broadly to include matters that would benefit a former employer if the official left that employer within the preceding two years. Hendricks' departure from Gulf South occurred in November 2022. The contract vote happened in January 2026—more than three years later.

The timing may place Hendricks outside the statutory conflict window. But the question of whether he actually recused himself remains unresolved.

What the minutes show

The Flightline reviewed the official minutes from the January 9, 2026, commission meeting, certified by County Clerk Denise Watkins. The Gulf South contract appears as item 7.4 on the consent agenda. The minutes state:

"Motion by Commissioner Hendricks to approve the consent agenda. Second by Commissioner Bergosh. Motion carried 4-1, Commissioner Martinez voting no."

There is no record of Hendricks recusing himself. No notation that he left the room. No abstention recorded.

Video of the meeting, available on the county's website, shows Hendricks seated at the dais throughout the consent agenda discussion. He does not speak during the brief mention of the Gulf South contract. When Board Chair Jeff Bergosh calls for the vote, Hendricks raises his hand with the majority.

Asked about the discrepancy between his account and the official record, Hendricks said the minutes were "incomplete."

"I told Jeff before the meeting I had a conflict," Hendricks said, referring to Bergosh. "Maybe the clerk didn't catch it. That's an administrative thing."

Bergosh did not respond to requests for comment. Clerk Watkins, through a spokesperson, said her office "accurately records all motions, seconds, and votes as they occur in the meeting."

The dissenting vote

Commissioner Rosa Martinez, the only no vote, said she opposed the contract because of the lack of competitive bidding—not because of Hendricks' former employment, which she said she did not know about at the time.

"My concern was process," Martinez said in an interview. "$2.1 million is not a small contract. We should be getting multiple bids on something that size. That's just good governance."

Martinez said she was "troubled" to learn of Hendricks' prior employment with Gulf South.

"I don't know if there's a legal violation there," she said. "But it doesn't look good. And the fact that he says he recused himself when the minutes say he didn't—that raises more questions."

Martinez said she would ask the county attorney to review the matter at the next board meeting, scheduled for April 23.

Gulf South's response

Gulf South Environmental Services did not respond to multiple requests for comment. A receptionist at the company's Mobile office said executives were "not available" and declined to provide an email address for the communications department.

State corporate records show Gulf South is a privately held company with annual revenues estimated between $15 million and $25 million. The company's registered agent is listed as a Mobile law firm, Hartley & Associates, which also did not respond to inquiries.

What happens next

Florida's Commission on Ethics has jurisdiction over complaints involving potential conflicts of interest by local officials. A complaint can be filed by any resident of the state. The commission does not comment on whether complaints are pending.

The county's Office of Inspector General, created in 2019 after a series of procurement scandals, can also investigate potential contracting irregularities. Inspector General Sandra Pittman said her office had not opened an investigation into the Gulf South contract but would "review any concerns brought to our attention."

The contract itself is already in effect. Gulf South began providing services under the agreement on February 1, 2026, according to county records. The first quarterly payment of $175,000 was issued on April 3.

Hendricks, asked if he would support reopening the contract for competitive bidding, said no.

"The county is getting good service at a fair price," he said. "Revisiting this would just waste more taxpayer money on a bureaucratic exercise."

Martinez disagreed.

"The taxpayers deserve to know they're getting the best deal," she said. "And right now, we can't tell them that."

articles.label: Development
articles.headline: Downtown Pensacola's empty storefronts: Who owns them and why they stay dark
articles.dek: A block-by-block look at Palafox Street vacancies reveals out-of-state LLCs, stalled permits, and rents that local businesses say make no sense.
brief: At least 23 ground-floor commercial spaces along Palafox Street between Garden and Main streets have sat vacant for six months or longer, according to a Flightline survey.
Property records show 14 of those spaces are owned by LLCs registered outside Florida, with addresses in Delaware, Georgia, and Texas.
Local business owners and commercial real estate agents say asking rents have climbed 40% since 2021 while foot traffic remains below pre-pandemic levels.
articles.body:
The paper sign in the window at 114 South Palafox has been there so long the sun bleached it white. "For Lease," it says. A phone number with a Georgia area code. The space behind the glass is empty except for a single folding chair and a tangle of extension cords.

Next door, same story. Across the street, three more.

Walk Palafox from Garden Street to Main on any Tuesday afternoon and you'll pass more dark windows than lit ones. The Flightline counted 23 ground-floor commercial vacancies along that seven-block stretch last week. That's not including spaces with active construction permits or those under contract. Twenty-three empty storefronts in what's supposed to be downtown Pensacola's commercial heart.

The question everyone asks: Why?

The answers, according to property records, permit filings, and interviews with local business owners and real estate agents, point in several directions at once. Out-of-state ownership. Asking rents disconnected from local economics. A permit process that frustrates prospective tenants. And in at least a few cases, owners who seem content to wait.

Following the deeds

Escambia County property records tell part of the story.

Of the 23 vacant storefronts identified in the Flightline survey, 14 are owned by limited liability companies registered outside Florida. Six trace back to Delaware—a state whose corporate privacy laws make identifying actual owners difficult. Four are registered in Georgia. Two in Texas. Two more list addresses in Alabama.

The remaining nine are owned by Florida-based LLCs or individuals. Of those, four are held by the same family trust, the Gonzalez Family Holdings LLC, which purchased the properties between 2017 and 2019.

Maria Reyes, a commercial real estate agent with Studer Properties who specializes in downtown listings, said the ownership pattern has shifted dramatically over the past decade.

"In 2015, I could call most landlords directly. I knew them. They lived here," Reyes said. "Now I'm leaving voicemails with property management companies in Atlanta. Sometimes I never hear back."

The Flightline attempted to contact owners or representatives for all 23 properties. Eight did not respond. Six declined to comment. Representatives for four properties returned calls but would not discuss specific rental terms or vacancy timelines.

One did.

'The math doesn't work'

The building at 220 South Palafox has been vacant since October 2024, when a gift shop called Coastal Finds closed after three years. The 1,800-square-foot space is owned by Magnolia Street Partners LLC, registered in Atlanta.

James Chen, a property manager with the firm, said the asking rent is $32 per square foot annually, triple net—meaning the tenant pays taxes, insurance, and maintenance on top of base rent. For 220 South Palafox, that works out to roughly $4,800 per month before utilities and buildout costs.

"That's consistent with the market," Chen said.

Local business owners disagree.

Sarah Whitfield opened a ceramics studio on East Intendencia Street four years ago. She looked at spaces on Palafox in 2022 before settling on a side street location with rent at $18 per square foot.

"The math doesn't work," Whitfield said. "You're a small business. You're not pulling Gallery Night traffic every day. You need rent you can cover in your slow months."

Whitfield said she was quoted $28 per square foot for a Palafox location in 2022. She calculated she would need to nearly double her monthly revenue to break even.

"So I walked," she said. "And that space is still empty."

Data from the Pensacola Downtown Improvement Board shows average asking rents for ground-floor commercial space on Palafox climbed from $22 per square foot in 2021 to $31 in 2025. Foot traffic counts taken at Palafox and Romana during Gallery Night and regular Saturdays show attendance still roughly 15% below 2019 levels.

The gap between rent expectations and local business reality has become a running joke among downtown entrepreneurs, said Marcus Webb, who owns a coffee shop on Jefferson Street.

"Everyone knows those spaces are empty," Webb said. "Everyone knows why. The landlords are waiting for some chain restaurant or a bank branch. They're not looking for us."

The permit pile

Rent isn't the only barrier.

Three of the vacant Palafox storefronts have permit applications on file with the City of Pensacola's Development Services Department—applications that have been pending for more than six months.

One, at 156 South Palafox, involves a proposed restaurant. The permit application was submitted in August 2025. A city spokesperson said the application has been through two rounds of revision requests related to fire suppression requirements and ADA compliance. The applicant, a Jacksonville-based restaurant group, did not respond to requests for comment.

Another, at 201 South Palafox, involves converting a former retail space into a fitness studio. That application has been pending since September 2025. The proposed tenant, Pensacola resident Andre Williams, said he's been waiting on approval for changes to the building's HVAC system.

"I'm paying holding costs on a lease for a space I can't open," Williams said. "Every month that goes by, I'm asking myself if this was a mistake."

City spokesperson Jennifer Martinez said the permitting timeline depends on the complexity of the project and how quickly applicants respond to revision requests. She said the average commercial permit in 2025 took 67 days from submission to approval, but projects requiring building modifications averaged 112 days.

"We work with applicants to move things forward," Martinez said. "But we have to ensure compliance with building codes."

The waiting game

Some vacancies appear to have no near-term resolution.

The two-story building at 130 South Palafox has been empty since 2021, when a clothing boutique closed during the pandemic. Property records show the building sold in March 2022 to an LLC registered in Wilmington, Delaware. The purchase price was $1.2 million.

A faded leasing sign in the window lists a phone number that rings to a voicemail box. The Flightline called three times over two weeks. No one returned the calls.

Permit records show no applications for the property since the sale. The building appears on the city's commercial vacancy list, which is shared quarterly with the Downtown Improvement Board.

Donnie Harper, executive director of the Downtown Improvement Board, said there's no mechanism to compel owners to lease or sell vacant properties.

"We can encourage. We can facilitate. We can't force," Harper said. "If someone wants to sit on a building, that's their right."

Harper said the board has discussed incentive programs—façade improvement grants, rent subsidies for local entrepreneurs—but funding remains limited. The board's annual budget is roughly $800,000, most of it dedicated to maintenance and events.

"We're competing with cities that have real resources," Harper said. "Pensacola punches above its weight on a lot of things. This is one area where we're behind."

What comes next

Escambia County Commissioner Lumon May, whose district includes downtown, said he's heard complaints about vacancies from constituents for years. He said he's open to discussing policy interventions but cautioned against overreach.

"Property rights matter," May said. "But so does a vibrant downtown. We need to find a balance."

Options that have worked in other cities include vacancy taxes, which penalize owners who leave commercial spaces empty for extended periods. Philadelphia implemented one in 2020. Washington, D.C., has had a version since 2011. Both cities have seen modest reductions in commercial vacancy rates, according to studies published by their respective planning departments.

Pensacola City Council has not formally discussed such measures. Council President Allison Patton said she would be willing to "explore the data" but stopped short of endorsing any specific approach.

"I hear the frustration," Patton said. "I also know these are complicated issues with property rights implications. We need to be thoughtful."

For now, the storefronts stay dark.

On a Thursday evening in early April, the 100 block of South Palafox was quiet. A couple walked past 114 South Palafox and paused to look through the window at the empty room inside.

"What used to be here?" one asked.

"I don't remember," the other said.

They kept walking.

articles.label: Community
articles.headline: Blue Angels homecoming week brings 200,000 visitors. Pensacola Beach residents brace for the other side of the airshow.
articles.dek: The July practice schedule means six weeks of jet noise, packed roads, and short-term rentals at capacity. Some locals cash in. Others leave town entirely.
brief: The Blue Angels homecoming airshow draws an estimated 200,000 visitors to Pensacola Beach each July, generating roughly $85 million in regional economic impact
Pensacola Beach residents report Tuesday and Wednesday practice sessions create sustained noise levels and traffic congestion from late May through mid-July
Short-term rental bookings on the beach hit 94% capacity during airshow week, while some year-round residents schedule vacations to avoid the crowds
articles.body:
The first practice run comes without warning. A Tuesday morning in late May, somewhere around 11:30, and the roar splits the sky over Pensacola Beach like fabric tearing. Car alarms trigger in the Margaritaville parking lot. A toddler on the beach starts crying. Tourists crane their necks and point. Residents don't look up.

They already know what's coming.

The Blue Angels homecoming airshow, scheduled this year for July 10-11, draws an estimated 200,000 visitors to the Pensacola Beach area across the event weekend. The Pensacola Bay Area Convention and Visitors Bureau projects $85 million in regional economic impact from lodging, dining, retail and transportation spending. Hotels from Gulf Breeze to downtown Pensacola report sellouts months in advance.

What the tourism numbers don't capture is the six-week prelude. The practice schedule. The Tuesday and Wednesday sessions that begin in late May and continue through the show itself. For year-round residents of Pensacola Beach—a community of roughly 3,500 people living on a barrier island designed to accommodate 40,000 on a summer Saturday—the airshow isn't a weekend event. It's a season.

The math of a barrier island

Pensacola Beach has one road in and one road out. The Bob Sikes Bridge connects the island to Gulf Breeze, funneling every vehicle through a single toll plaza. On a typical summer weekend, traffic backs up past the Pensacola Beach Boulevard intersection. During airshow week, the backup extends onto U.S. Highway 98.

"I've timed it at two hours and forty minutes from the foot of the bridge to my driveway," said Margaret Thornton, who has lived on Ariola Drive since 2003. "That's three miles. I could walk faster, except you can't walk on the bridge."

The Santa Rosa Island Authority, which manages beach operations, coordinates with Escambia County and the Florida Highway Patrol on traffic control during major events. SRIA Executive Director Paolo Ghio said the authority has implemented contraflow lanes on the bridge during peak exit times, but acknowledged the infrastructure constraints are fundamental.

"There's only so much you can do with one bridge," Ghio said. "We're looking at everything from expanded water taxi service to remote parking in Gulf Breeze with shuttle buses. But the reality is, this island was built for a certain capacity, and the airshow exceeds that capacity."

Practice makes permanent

The Blue Angels flight demonstration squadron has been based at Naval Air Station Pensacola since 1949. The team performs roughly 60 shows annually across the country, but the Pensacola homecoming holds particular significance—it's the only show where the pilots sleep in their own beds.

Training for the July show begins in earnest in late May. The practice sessions occur twice weekly, typically Tuesday and Wednesday, starting around 11:30 a.m. and lasting approximately 45 minutes. Six F/A-18 Super Hornets execute the same maneuvers they'll perform for the crowd, flying patterns that carry them directly over the residential areas of Pensacola Beach, Gulf Breeze, and downtown Pensacola.

The Navy does not publish decibel measurements for the practice sessions, but independent readings taken by residents and reviewed by The Flightline show peak levels between 115 and 130 decibels during low-altitude passes. For comparison, a chainsaw at three feet registers around 110 decibels. Sustained exposure above 85 decibels can cause hearing damage.

"You can't have a phone conversation," said David Chen, who works remotely from his home on Via de Luna. "You can't do a video call. For about an hour, twice a week, from Memorial Day to the second week of July, my workday just stops."

Chen said he's adjusted by blocking those windows on his calendar. Others haven't found workarounds as easily.

Linda Caruso operates a dog grooming business out of her home near the public beach access. She said the practice sessions send the animals into panic.

"I had a golden retriever break a tooth trying to get out of a crate," Caruso said. "Now I just don't book appointments on those days. That's eight days of lost income over the summer."

The rental calculation

Not everyone on Pensacola Beach dreads the airshow. For property owners who rent their homes short-term, July represents the peak of the peak.

Vacation rental data from AirDNA, a short-term rental analytics firm, shows Pensacola Beach properties averaging $412 per night during airshow week in 2025, compared to $287 per night during a typical July week. Occupancy rates hit 94%, the highest of any week in the calendar year.

Tom and Rebecca Hollister own a three-bedroom home on Panferio Drive that they purchased in 2019 specifically as a rental property. They live in Birmingham, Alabama, and visit the home perhaps twice a year. The airshow week alone generates roughly $8,500 in gross rental income.

"We love the Blue Angels," Rebecca Hollister said. "It's part of why we bought there. The airshow is what makes that week special."

The Hollisters said they've never experienced a practice session. They typically visit in October, after hurricane season.

The tension between year-round residents and investment property owners has simmered on Pensacola Beach for years, predating the airshow debate. A 2024 survey by the Santa Rosa Island Authority found that 61% of residential properties on the beach are now registered as short-term rentals. The percentage has increased every year since 2018.

"There's a point where you stop being a neighborhood and start being a hotel district," said James Worthington, president of the Pensacola Beach Advocates, a residents' association. "The airshow is great for the hotel district. The people who actually live here have a different perspective."

The exodus

Some residents have found their own solution. They leave.

Patricia Odom has lived on Pensacola Beach since 1987. She remembers when the airshow was a single-day event with modest crowds. She remembers when you could drive to Publix on a Saturday morning in July without planning your route around traffic.

Now, every year, she books a cabin in the North Carolina mountains for the last two weeks of June and the first two weeks of July.

"I time it around the practice schedule," Odom said. "I leave before Memorial Day weekend and come back after the show. It's the only way I can enjoy my own home."

Odom said at least a dozen of her neighbors do the same thing. A informal network has formed, sharing information about the practice schedule—which the Navy announces only a few weeks in advance—and coordinating departure dates.

"We joke that we're climate refugees," she said. "Except it's not the climate we're running from."

The economic argument

Defenders of the airshow point to its economic impact as justification for the disruption. The $85 million figure cited by the tourism bureau represents spending across the entire Pensacola metro area, not just the beach. Hotels, restaurants, gas stations and retailers throughout Escambia and Santa Rosa counties see increased traffic during airshow week.

Visit Pensacola President Dana Young called the homecoming show "the signature event that defines our destination."

"The Blue Angels are synonymous with Pensacola," Young said. "When people think of this community, they think of the Blue Angels. That brand recognition has value that extends far beyond one weekend."

The Navy's presence in Pensacola extends well beyond the flight demonstration squadron. Naval Air Station Pensacola employs approximately 16,000 military and civilian personnel and contributes an estimated $4.6 billion annually to the regional economy. The Blue Angels represent a fraction of that footprint, but serve as the most visible symbol of the military-community relationship.

Escambia County Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, whose district includes Pensacola Beach, said the economic benefits outweigh the temporary inconveniences.

"Six weeks of practice sessions is a small price to pay for what the airshow brings to this community," Bergosh said. "And let's be honest—if you bought a house on Pensacola Beach, you knew the Blue Angels were part of the deal."

The question nobody answers

What would happen if residents pushed back? If the practice sessions moved elsewhere, or the homecoming show relocated to a less congested venue?

The Navy has never indicated any interest in changing the arrangement. NAS Pensacola's public affairs office did not respond to questions about whether alternative practice locations had ever been considered.

The reality is that few residents are asking for the airshow to go away entirely. The complaints center on mitigation—traffic management, noise abatement, better communication about schedules.

"I don't want to kill the airshow," said Margaret Thornton, the Ariola Drive resident. "I just want someone to acknowledge that it costs something. That the people who live here year-round are subsidizing an event that mostly benefits people who don't."

The Santa Rosa Island Authority has discussed creating a resident liaison position to coordinate between the beach community and event organizers. The position remains unfunded in the current budget.

Meanwhile, the calendar advances. Memorial Day approaches. The first practice session will arrive without warning, on a Tuesday morning in late May, somewhere around 11:30.

The tourists will point at the sky. The residents won't look up. They'll already be packing for the mountains.
